topic_title: Case study: Automated medical triage — what failure mode was tolerated?

sequence_drift.notes: All terms used without definition are explicitly covered in prerequisites (9.5 automation bias, 9.7-9.9 Judgment Framework Q1/Q2/Q3, 9.11 high-stakes domains, 10.1 pipeline/override-as-theater/calibration/training-data). Newly introduced terms (failure mode, systematic under-scoring, sub-group performance audit, deterioration-contradiction alert, evaluation gap, override accountability, post-market surveillance analogy) are all defined on first use. Domain examples in section 7 (credit scoring, hiring algorithms) are illustrative analogies, not forward-taught concepts.

topic_scope.notes: Corpus stays tightly within headline scope: what the triage system is, the specific failure mode (systematic under-scoring), the four governance gaps and automation bias that allowed it to persist, Judgment Framework application to the case, and the earliest-detection controls. The post-market surveillance analogy in section 7 is one-sentence contextual background listed in concepts_introduced. Mention of credit scoring and hiring in section 7 is a single sentence making a generalizability point without teaching those cases.

beginner_readability.notes: All non-everyday terms defined on first use or explicitly back-referenced to prior topics with topic numbers. No paragraphs introduce 3+ new terms at once. No hedging language detected. Sentence register is direct and beginner-accessible throughout. Acronym 'AI' is curriculum-established from M2 with no new unexplained acronyms introduced.

verdict: PASS. All six checks pass. Required sections present and substantive (section 5 ~1980w, section 4 ~280w, section 10 ~220w).
